```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
winners("Daniel"; "Gabriel"; "Roland"; "Vincent").
price(250; 260; 270; 280).
order(3; 4; 7; 8).

% Each winner has exactly one price and one order.
{auction(W, P, O) : price(P), order(O)} = 1 :- winners(W).

% Each price is used exactly once.
{auction(W, P, O) : winners(W), order(O)} = 1 :- price(P).

% Each order is used exactly once.
{auction(W, P, O) : winners(W), price(P)} = 1 :- order(O).

% 1. The butterfly auctioned 7th sold for 20 dollars less than the insect won by Daniel.
:- auction("Daniel", P_Daniel, _), auction(_, P_7th, 7), P_7th != P_Daniel - 20.

% 2.1 The four butterflies — the insect that sold for $260, the butterfly that was auctioned 4th, the butterfly that was auctioned 7th, and the butterfly won by Roland — are all different.
% This means the four distinct entities must correspond to four distinct auction entries.
% We enforce that these four are not the same as each other via uniqueness constraints.
% Instead of counting, we use the fact that they must be different in their attributes.

% We ensure that the item sold for $260, the 4th lot, the 7th lot, and Roland's lot are all different.
% So: if any two of these are the same, it's invalid.

% Check: if the $260 item is the 4th lot → conflict unless they are same entity, but they must be different.
% So we must enforce that the 4th lot ≠ $260 item unless they are the same, but we need all four to be different.

% Instead, we use: the four objects (by attribute) must be distinct.
% We can express: for each pair among these four, they are not the same auction entry.

% Let’s define: 
%   A: the auction entry with price 260
%   B: the auction entry with order 4
%   C: the auction entry with order 7
%   D: the auction entry with winner Roland

% We require A ≠ B, A ≠ C, A ≠ D, B ≠ C, B ≠ D, C ≠ D.

% So we add constraints to prevent any two of these from being the same auction.

% The auction entry with price 260 is not the same as the one with order 4
:- auction(W1, 260, O1), auction(W2, _, 4), W1=W2, O1=4.

% The auction entry with price 260 is not the same as the one with order 7
:- auction(W1, 260, O1), auction(W2, _, 7), W1=W2, O1=7.

% The auction entry with price 260 is not the same as Roland's lot
:- auction(W1, 260, O1), auction("Roland", _, _), W1="Roland", O1=O1.

% The auction entry with order 4 is not the same as order 7
:- auction(W1, _, 4), auction(W2, _, 7), W1=W2.

% The auction entry with order 4 is not Roland's lot
:- auction(W1, _, 4), auction("Roland", _, _), W1="Roland".

% The auction entry with order 7 is not Roland's lot
:- auction(W1, _, 7), auction("Roland", _, _), W1="Roland".

% 2.2 The insect that sold for $260 is one of: the butterfly that was auctioned 4th, the butterfly that was auctioned 7th, or the butterfly won by Roland.
% So: if P=260, then O=4 or O=7 or W="Roland"
:- auction(W, 260, O), O != 4, O != 7, W != "Roland".

% 2.3 The butterfly that was auctioned 4th is one of: the insect that sold for $260, the butterfly that was auctioned 7th, or the butterfly won by Roland.
% So: if O=4, then P=260 or O=7 or W="Roland"
% But O=4 and O=7 cannot be true at the same time → so "O=7" is false when O=4.
% So: if O=4, then P=260 or W="Roland"
:- auction(W, P, 4), P != 260, W != "Roland".

% 3. The butterfly won by Gabriel was the 8th lot.
:- auction(W, _, O), W="Gabriel", O != 8.
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
names("Angela"; "Edmund"; "Homer"; "Irene").
price(4; 5; 6; 7).
drink("cream soda"; "iced tea"; "root beer"; "water").

% Each person has exactly one drink and one price.
{order(N, D, P) : drink(D), price(P)} = 1 :- names(N).

% Each drink is used exactly once.
{order(N1, D, P1) : names(N1), price(P1)} = 1 :- drink(D).

% Each price is used exactly once.
{order(N1, D1, P) : names(N1), drink(D1)} = 1 :- price(P).

% 1. Edmund paid 1 dollar less than the one who got the water.
:- order("Edmund", _, P1), order(_, "water", P2), P1 != P2 - 1.

% 2. The diner who paid $4 had the iced tea.
:- order(N, D, 4), D != "iced tea".

% 3. Angela paid more than the one who got the cream soda.
:- order("Angela", _, P1), order(_, "cream soda", P2), P1 <= P2.

% 4.1 The diner who paid $5 and the one who got the iced tea are different.
:- order(N, "iced tea", 5).

% 4.2 The diner who paid $5 got the cream soda or got the iced tea.
:- order(N, D, 5), D != "cream soda", D != "iced tea".

% 4.3 The one who got the iced tea got the cream soda or got the iced tea.
% This is always true since "iced tea" is one of the options, so redundant.
% But to be explicit: if someone got iced tea, then their drink is either cream soda or iced tea.
% So we just need to ensure that if D = "iced tea", then D is in {cream soda, iced tea} — always true.
% So no constraint needed.

% 5. The four diners were the diner who paid $5, the one who got the iced tea, the one who got the root beer, and Irene.
% This means: the set {diner who paid $5, diner who got iced tea, diner who got root beer, Irene} has exactly 4 distinct people.
% So: the four individuals are all different, and cover all four people.

% We enforce that the four entities mentioned are distinct and cover all names.
% We can express this by saying: the set of people who paid $5, got iced tea, got root beer, or are Irene must be exactly the four names.

% Instead, we use: each of the four roles must be assigned to a different person, and collectively they cover all four.

% We use the following: the four individuals: 
%   - the one who paid $5
%   - the one who got iced tea
%   - the one who got root beer
%   - Irene
% must be all different, and since there are 4 people, they must be exactly the four names.

% So: the four roles are distinct.
:- order(N1, _, 5), order(N2, "iced tea", _), N1 = N2.
:- order(N1, _, 5), order(N3, "root beer", _), N1 = N3.
:- order(N1, _, 5), N1 = "Irene".
:- order(N2, "iced tea", _), order(N3, "root beer", _), N2 = N3.
:- order(N2, "iced tea", _), N2 = "Irene".
:- order(N3, "root beer", _), N3 = "Irene".

% Now, we can also add that the four people mentioned must be all names — but since we have only four names and they are all distinct, this is enforced.
% So the above constraints ensure that the four roles are assigned to four different people, i.e., all names are covered.

% Final: ensure that the assignment is unique.
% Clingo will find the unique solution.
```

```asp
:- "Hannah" = "Ginger".  % Trivially false, but ensures they are distinct.

:- match("Hannah", P, M), P != 180, M != "Lynda".
```

```asp
:- #count {I, C : discover(I, Y, C)} != 1, year(Y).
:- #count {I, Y : discover(I, Y, C)} != 1, culture(C).

% 1. Jujihm was discovered in 1768.
:- discover("Jujihm", Y, _), Y != 1768.

% 2. Verinya was discovered in 1761.
:- discover("Verinya", Y, _), Y != 1761.

% 3. Jujihm was discovered 14 years after the island on which the Wainani people lived.
% So the Wainani island was discovered in 1768 - 14 = 1754.
:- discover("Jujihm", Y, _), Y != 1768.
:- discover(I, Y, "Wainani"), Y != 1754.

% 4. The island on which the Kukani people lived was discovered 7 years after Fushil.
% So Y_Kukani = Y_Fushil + 7.
% We enforce this via: if Fushil has year Y, then Kukani island has year Y+7.
:- discover("Fushil", Y, _), discover(I, Y1, "Kukani"), Y1 != Y + 7.

:- discover("Fushil", 1768, _).

% So either the 1768 island has culture Hakili, or Fushil has culture Hakili.
% But Fushil ≠ 1768 (from 5.1), so if Fushil has Hakili, that’s fine, but the 1768 island must have Hakili or Fushil is Hakili.
% But Fushil is not 1768, so the 1768 island must have Hakili.
% So: if an island is discovered in 1768 and it's not Fushil, then it must have culture Hakili.
% But we already know Jujihm is 1768, and Fushil ≠ 1768, so Jujihm must have Hakili.
% So we enforce: the island discovered in 1768 must have culture Hakili.
:- discover(I, 1768, C), C != "Hakili".

% 5.3 Fushil is the island on which the Hakili people lived OR the island discovered in 1768.
% So either Fushil has culture Hakili, or Fushil was discovered in 1768.
% But from 5.1, Fushil ≠ 1768 → so Fushil must have culture Hakili.
:- discover("Fushil", Y, C), C != "Hakili".
```

```asp
names("Cornick"; "Dreadco"; "Foltron"; "Worul").
months(1; 2; 3; 4).
companies("Permias"; "Rubicorp"; "Techtrin"; "Ubersplore").

% Core relation: launches(Company, Rocket, Month)
% Each company, rocket, and month appears exactly once.
% Use choice and cardinality constraints to enforce bijections.

% Each company is assigned exactly one rocket and one month
{ launches(C, N, M) : names(N), months(M) } = 1 :- companies(C).

% Each rocket is launched in exactly one month
{ launches(C, N, M) : companies(C) } = 1 :- names(N), months(M).

% Each month has exactly one rocket launch
{ launches(C, N, M) : companies(C), names(N) } = 1 :- months(M).

% Clue 1: The Worul, the rocket that will launch in February, and the rocket that will launch in January are all different rockets.
% So Worul cannot be launched in January or February.
:- launches(_, "Worul", 1).
:- launches(_, "Worul", 2).

% Clue 2: The Dreadco is made by Ubersplore.
% So Ubersplore must launch Dreadco.
launches("Ubersplore", "Dreadco", M) :- months(M).

% Clue 3: The rocket developed by Permias will launch 1 month before the Foltron.
% So there exists M such that Permias launches in M and Foltron launches in M+1.
% We enforce: if Permias launches in M (M < 4), then Foltron must launch in M+1.
% This ensures the "1 month before" condition.
:- launches("Permias", _, M), M < 4, not launches(_, "Foltron", M+1).

% Clue 4: The rocket that will launch in January is made by Techtrin.
% So Techtrin must launch in January.
launches("Techtrin", N, 1) :- names(N).
```

```asp
% Define the constants
wingspan(102; 106; 110; 114).
name("Merlin"; "Pepper"; "Spike"; "Sunshine").
age(4; 5; 8; 9).

% Each condor has exactly one name, one age, and one wingspan
% Use choice rules to generate possible assignments
assign(N, A, W) :- name(N), age(A), wingspan(W).

% Ensure each name, age, and wingspan is used exactly once
:- #count{N, A, W : assign(N, A, W), N = N1} > 1 : name(N1).
:- #count{N, A, W : assign(N, A, W), A = A1} > 1 : age(A1).
:- #count{N, A, W : assign(N, A, W), W = W1} > 1 : wingspan(W1).

% Clue 1: Sunshine is either the condor with a wingspan of 102 inches or the 5-year-old bird.
% So: either assign("Sunshine", 5, W) for some W, or assign("Sunshine", A, 102) for some A.
% If Sunshine is not 5 years old and does not have 102-inch wingspan, then invalid.
:- assign("Sunshine", A, W), A != 5, W != 102.

% Clue 2: The condor with a wingspan of 114 inches is 5 years old.
:- assign(N, A, W), W = 114, A != 5.

% Clue 3: Pepper has a wingspan of 114 inches.
:- assign(N, A, W), N = "Pepper", W != 114.

% Clue 4: The four condors are the 9-year-old bird, Pepper, Merlin, and the condor with a wingspan of 106 inches.
% This means the 9-year-old bird, Pepper, Merlin, and the 106-inch wingspan bird are the four distinct condors.
% Since all names and values are used exactly once, we need to ensure that:
% - The 9-year-old bird exists (true by uniqueness)
% - Pepper exists (true)
% - Merlin exists (true)
% - The 106-inch wingspan bird exists (true)
% But the clue implies that these four are the only condors, so we must ensure that:
% The 9-year-old bird is not Pepper or Merlin unless they are 9, but that’s allowed.
% However, the clue is listing four entities, so we must ensure that the 106-inch wingspan bird is one of the condors — which is enforced.
% But there's a subtle point: the clue says "the four condors are" these four, so we must ensure that:
% The 9-year-old bird is not the same as Pepper or Merlin unless they are 9, but that’s okay.
% So no additional constraint is needed beyond uniqueness.

% Clue 5: The 9-year-old bird has a wingspan 4 inches shorter than the 4-year-old bird.
% So: wingspan(9-year-old) = wingspan(4-year-old) - 4
% We can write:
:- assign(N1, 9, W1), assign(N2, 4, W2), W1 != W2 - 4.
```

```asp
{released(G, M, D) : games(G), developers(D)} = 1 :- months(M).

% So: M(Angry Ants) = M(Gadingo's game) + 3
:- released(G, M1, "Gadingo"), released("Angry Ants", M2, _), M2 != M1 + 3.

% Clue 3: The game released by Apptastic was released 3 months later than Flowcarts.
% So: M(Apptastic's game) = M(Flowcarts) + 3
:- released("Flowcarts", M1, _), released(G, M2, "Apptastic"), M2 != M1 + 3.

% Clue 4.1: The game released by Novio and the game released in July are different.
% So: The game by Novio ≠ the game released in July
:- released(G, M, "Novio"), released(G, 7, _).

% Clue 4.2: The game released by Novio is the game released in July or Bubble Bombs.
% So: M = 7 OR G = "Bubble Bombs"
:- released(G, M, "Novio"), M != 7, G != "Bubble Bombs".

% Clue 4.3: The game released in July is the game released by Apptastic or Bubble Bombs.
% So: D = "Apptastic" OR G = "Bubble Bombs"
:- released(G, 7, D), D != "Apptastic", G != "Bubble Bombs".

% Clue 5.1: Peter Parka and the app released by Novio are different.
% So: Peter Parka ≠ the game by Novio
:- released("Peter Parka", _, _), released("Peter Parka", _, "Novio").

% Clue 5.2: Peter Parka is the app released by Novio or the game released in May.
% So: D = "Novio" OR M = 5
:- released("Peter Parka", M, D), D != "Novio", M != 5.

% Clue 5.3: The app released by Novio is Peter Parka or the game released in May.
% So: G = "Peter Parka" OR M = 5
:- released(G, M, "Novio"), G != "Peter Parka", M != 5.

% Clue 6: The game released in July was made by Apptastic.
% So: If M = 7, then D = Apptastic
:- released(G, 7, D), D != "Apptastic".
```

```asp
% Define the constants in each category.
players("Awad"; "Daregh"; "Gardelli"; "Rothvum").
years(1976; 1979; 1982; 1985).
positions("center back"; "center forward"; "goalie"; "sweeper").

% Each player has exactly one year and one position.
1 { player_data(P, Y, Pos) : years(Y), positions(Pos) } 1 :- players(P).

% Each year is used exactly once.
1 { player_data(P, Y, Pos) : players(P), positions(Pos) } 1 :- years(Y).

% Each position is used exactly once.
1 { player_data(P, Y, Pos) : players(P), years(Y) } 1 :- positions(Pos).

% 1. Daregh started 6 years before the goalie.
:- player_data("Daregh", Y1, _), player_data(_, Y2, "goalie"), Y2 - Y1 != 6.

% 2.1 The player who started in 1982 and Daregh are different.
:- player_data("Daregh", 1982, _), player_data(P, 1982, _), P != "Daregh".

% 2.2 The player who started in 1982 was the goalie or Daregh.
:- player_data(P, 1982, Pos), P != "Daregh", Pos != "goalie".

% 3. Awad began playing in 1985.
:- player_data("Awad", Y, _), Y != 1985.

% 4. The player who started in 1976 was the sweeper.
:- player_data(P, 1976, Pos), Pos != "sweeper".

% 5.1 Gardelli and the player who started in 1979 are different.
:- player_data("Gardelli", 1979, _), player_data(P, 1979, _), P != "Gardelli".

% 5.2 Gardelli was the center back or the player who started in 1979.
% Since Gardelli ≠ the player who started in 1979 (from 5.1), Gardelli must be center back.
:- player_data("Gardelli", Y, Pos), Pos != "center back".

% 5.3 The player who started in 1979 was the center back or the player who started in 1979.
% This is tautological: the player who started in 1979 is the player who started in 1979.
% So it's always true — no need to encode.
```

```asp
% No two people share the same age or state.
:- centenarian(N1, A, S1), centenarian(N2, A, S2), N1 != N2.
:- centenarian(N1, A1, S), centenarian(N2, A2, S), N1 != N2.

% 1. Kyle is 1 year older than Arlene.

% 2. Kyle is not 109 or 110 years old.
:- centenarian("Kyle", 109, _).
:- centenarian("Kyle", 110, _).

% 3. The Pennsylvania native is older than Ernesto.
:- centenarian("Ernesto", A1, _), centenarian(_, A2, "Pennsylvania"), A2 =< A1.

% 4. Ernesto is older than the Louisiana native.
:- centenarian("Ernesto", A1, _), centenarian(_, A2, "Louisiana"), A1 =< A2.

% 5. The person who is 111 years old is a native of South Dakota.
:- centenarian(_, 111, S), S != "South Dakota".
```

```asp
order(3; 4; 7; 8).

% Each winner has exactly one price and one order.

% Each price is used exactly once.

% Each order is used exactly once.
{auction(W, P, O) : winners(W), price(P)} = 1 :- order(O).

:- auction("Daniel", P_D, _), auction(_, P_7, 7), P_7 != P_D - 20.

% 2.1 The four butterflies — the insect that sold for $260, the butterfly that was auctioned 4th, the butterfly that was auctioned 7th, and the butterfly won by Roland — are all different.
% This means the four distinct auction entries must be pairwise different.
% We enforce that no two of these four are the same auction entry.

% $260 item ≠ 4th lot
:- auction(W, 260, O), auction(W, _, 4), O = 4.

% $260 item ≠ 7th lot
:- auction(W, 260, O), auction(W, _, 7), O = 7.

% $260 item ≠ Roland's lot
:- auction(W, 260, O), auction("Roland", _, _), W = "Roland".

% 4th lot ≠ 7th lot
:- auction(W, _, 4), auction(W, _, 7).

% 4th lot ≠ Roland's lot
:- auction(W, _, 4), auction("Roland", _, _), W = "Roland".

% 7th lot ≠ Roland's lot
:- auction(W, _, 7), auction("Roland", _, _), W = "Roland".

% 2.2 The insect that sold for $260 is one of: the butterfly that was auctioned 4th, the butterfly that was auctioned 7th, or the butterfly won by Roland.
```

```asp
:- #count {I, C : discover(I, Y, C)} != 1, year(Y).
:- #count {I, Y : discover(I, Y, C)} != 1, culture(C).
```

```asp
% 2. Verinya was discovered in 1761.
discover("Verinya", 1761, C) :- culture(C).

% 3. Jujihm was discovered 14 years after the island on which the Wainani people lived.
% So the Wainani island was discovered in 1768 - 14 = 1754.
discover(I, 1754, "Wainani") :- islands(I).

% 4. The island on which the Kukani people lived was discovered 7 years after Fushil.
% So: Y_Kukani = Y_Fushil + 7.
% We enforce this by checking that if Fushil is discovered in Y, then the Kukani island is discovered in Y+7.
% Since we have only four years, possible pairs: (1754,1761), (1761,1768), (1768,1775).
% But Jujihm is 1768, Verinya is 1761, so Fushil cannot be 1761 or 1768 (since years are unique).
% So Fushil must be 1754 ⇒ Kukani island discovered in 1761 ⇒ Verinya.
% So Verinya has culture Kukani.
discover("Verinya", 1761, "Kukani").

% 5.1 The island discovered in 1768 and Fushil are different.
% Jujihm is discovered in 1768, so Fushil ≠ Jujihm → already true by distinct islands.
% But we must ensure Fushil is not discovered in 1768.
:- discover("Fushil", 1768, _).

% 5.2 The island discovered in 1768 is the island on which the Hakili people lived OR Fushil.
% Since Fushil ≠ 1768 (from 5.1), the 1768 island (Jujihm) must have culture Hakili.
discover("Jujihm", 1768, "Hakili").

% 5.3 Fushil is the island on which the Hakili people lived OR the island discovered in 1768.
% Since Fushil ≠ 1768 (from 5.1), Fushil must have culture Hakili.
discover("Fushil", Y, "Hakili") :- year(Y).

% Now, the remaining island is Nuhirk, and remaining year is 1775, remaining culture is Manika'i.
discover("Nuhirk", 1775, "Manika'i").
```
